title: Managing large intermediate files
body:
Some very large files get written out during the process of running a simulation with verilator.  Specifically, I am seeing pairs of GCH files get written out which can add up to 100 MB or more for each run, but only for some testbenches.  As well as potentially hundreds of generated C++ files with a massive amount of redundancy.  With lots of tests (e.g. in a CI flow), this can eat up a lot of disk space and cause trouble (github actions has a storage limit of only 14 GB per runner).  Is there a way to automatically clean up all of the intermediate files during the build process such that only the executable needed to actually run the simulation is retained?

Also, I am using verilator with cocotb, so I'm not sure if this is an issue with verilator generally, or if it's something inherent to cocotb's process for building and running verilator.

On a related note, are there particular HDL constructs that result in Verilator has trouble with, resulting in mountains of generated code?  One relatively simple module that I ran under verilator (10G Ethernet MAC+PHY) produced about 180 generated C++ files, about 500 MB of temporary files in total and an output executable of 33 MB.  And it looks like the build was not hierarchical as all of the signal references are absolute, which is not a great sign as presumably multiple instances of the same core would result in many more copies of effectively the same intermediate code.  As the core in question here could be step-and-repeated in a design many times (order 10-100x) and combined with a lot of additional logic, this level of temporary file production for a single instance is worrying.
